params.text:
Dignity, respect and holistic needs

Promoting dignity and respect happens in the ordinary moments of care: knocking before entering, covering someone during personal care, speaking to — not over — the person, using their preferred name, and never rushing them.

People are more than their physical care needs. A holistic approach recognises the whole person across several dimensions:

  • Physical — health, mobility, comfort, pain and nutrition.
  • Emotional and mental — feelings, mood, self-esteem and wellbeing.
  • Social — relationships, family, friendships and community.
  • Cultural — language, customs, food and identity.
  • Spiritual and religious — faith, beliefs and what gives life meaning.

Under CQC Regulation 9, assessments should cover health, personal care, emotional, social, cultural, religious and spiritual needs.

params.question: A holistic, person-centred approach means looking after a person's emotional, social, cultural and spiritual needs as well as their physical care needs.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Holistic care deliberately looks beyond physical needs to include emotional, social, cultural and spiritual wellbeing.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Holistic care sees the whole person, not just their physical or medical needs.
